perturbation.original_text: The Parties agree that they shall not use any advantages derivable from such information in its own business or affairs, unless the same is done pursuant to a new agreeme nt with all other signatories to this document. Each signing party shall be held responsibl e and liable in case of a breach of this Agreement both in a profes sional and personal capacity.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties agree that they shall not use any advantages derivable from such information in its own business or affairs, unless the same is done pursuant to a new agreeme nt with all other signatories to this document. Each signing party shall be held liable in case of a breach of this Agreement in a professional capacity.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'personally' from the liability clause limits the potential damages recoverable in case of a breach. In many jurisdictions, piercing the corporate veil and holding individuals personally liable is possible under certain circumstances, especially for smaller companies or egregious violations. Removing this aspect weakens the deterrent effect and may contradict legal principles allowing for personal liability.
perturbation.law_explanation: While the agreement itself is not explicitly governed by California law, the principle of director liability (similar rules exist elsewhere) demonstrates that individuals *can* be held liable for corporate actions under specific conditions. Removing 'personal capacity' could be interpreted as an attempt to circumvent such liability, conflicting with the potential application of such laws.

CORPORATIONS [100 - 14707] ( Title 1 enacted by Stats. 1947, Ch. 1038. ) DIVISION 1. GENERAL CORPORATION LAW [100 - 2319] ( Division 1 repealed and added by Stats. 1975, Ch. 682. ) CHAPTER 3. Directors and Management [300 - 318] ( Chapter 3 added by Stats. 1975, Ch. 682. ) 316. (a) Subject to the provisions of Section 309, directors of a corporation who approve any of the following corporate actions shall be jointly and severally liable to the corporation for the benefit of all of the creditors or shareholders entitled to institute an action under subdivision (c): (1) The making of any distribution to its shareholders to the extent that it is contrary to the provisions of Sections 500 to 503, inclusive. (2) The distribution of assets to shareholders after institution of dissolution proceedings of the corporation, without paying or adequately providing for all known liabilities of the corporation, excluding any claims not filed by creditors within the time limit set by the court in a notice given to creditors under Chapters 18 (commencing with Section 1800), 19 (commencing with Section 1900) and 20 (commencing with Section 2000). (3) The making of any loan or guaranty contrary to Section 315. (b) A director who is present at a meeting of the boa
perturbation.justification: The modification removes 'personal capacity' from the liability clause, which weakens the potential for holding individuals personally liable for breaches. While the provided snippet from California Code, CORP 316, focuses on director liability for specific corporate actions, the broader principle it represents is that individuals can be held accountable for corporate actions under certain conditions. By removing 'personal capacity', the changed text attempts to limit liability, potentially conflicting with legal principles that allow for piercing the corporate veil and holding individuals personally liable, especially in cases of egregious violations or smaller companies. This aligns with the definition of Structural Flaws - Legal Contradiction, where the organization of a contract affects its enforceability.

perturbation.original_text: The receiving Party has the right to accept the information under this Agreeme nt or to decline the in formation in which case it will not be provided.
perturbation.changed_text: The receiving Party has the right to accept the information under this Agreeme nt or to decline the in formation in which case it will not be provided. However, if the receiving Party declines the information, they will still be bound by the non-compete provision.
perturbation.explanation: This creates a contradiction: a party can decline to receive confidential information, yet still be bound by a non-compete agreement that relies on the exchange of that information. This is unreasonable and likely unenforceable, as non-competes typically require consideration (something of value exchanged), and if no information is received, the consideration is questionable.
perturbation.law_explanation: This section of the Restatement clarifies the requirement of consideration for a contract to be valid. By binding a party to a non-compete without them receiving the confidential information (the consideration), this clause violates this fundamental principle of contract law.
perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a structural flaw that leads to a legal contradiction. The original text allows the receiving party to decline information. The changed text then binds the receiving party to a non-compete agreement even if they decline the information. This is contradictory because non-compete agreements typically require consideration (exchange of value), and if no information is received, the consideration is questionable, potentially rendering the non-compete unenforceable. This aligns with the 'Structural Flaws - Legal Contradiction' definition, as it creates a situation where the enforceability of the non-compete is undermined by the lack of consideration.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties both under stand that each Party makes no represe ntation or warranty as to the accuracy or completenes s of the information it provides. The Parties both agree that neither Party, nor any of its advisers, represe ntatives, agents, or employe es shall be held liable for utilization of Confidential Information which res ults from the other Party’s use of said information.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties both under stand that each Party makes no represe ntation or warranty as to the accuracy or completenes s of the information it provides. The Parties both agree that neither Party, nor any of its advisers, represe ntatives, agents, or employe es shall be held liable for utilization of Confidential Information which res ults from the other Party’s use of said information, unless such use was grossly negligent or willful.
perturbation.explanation: Adding "unless such use was grossly negligent or willful" shifts the burden of proof. The original text created a blanket liability waiver. This change suggests that liability might exist if the information was used in a grossly negligent or willful manner. This creates potential ambiguity that can be used in legal disputes.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UCC outlines how warranties can be disclaimed. Adding a condition like "unless grossly negligent or willful" introduces an ambiguity regarding the disclaimer's scope and effect. It contradicts the original intent of an absolute disclaimer by creating a loophole, potentially misleading parties about their rights and obligations.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-316. Exclusion or Modification of Warranties. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-316. Exclusion or Modification of Warranties. (1) Words or conduct relevant to the creation of an express warranty and words or conduct tending to negate or limit warranty shall be construed wherever reasonable as consistent with each other; but subject to the provisions of this Article on parol or extrinsic evidence (Section 2-202 ) negation or limitation is inoperative to the extent that such construction is unreasonable. (2)Subject to subsection (3), to exclude or modify the implied warranty of merchantability or any part of it the language must mention merchantability and in case of a writing must be conspicuous, and to exclude or modify any implied warranty of fitness the exclusion must be by a writing and conspicuous. Language to exclude all implied warranties of fitness is sufficient if it states, for example, that "There are no warranties which extend beyond the description on the face hereof." (3)Notwithstanding subsection (2) (a) unless the circumstances indicate otherwise, all implied warranties are excluded by expressions like "as is", "with all faults" or other language which in common understanding calls the buyer's attention to the exclusion of warranties and makes plain that there is no implied warranty; and (b) when the buyer before entering into the contract has examined the goods or the sample or model as fully as he desired or has refused to examine the goods there is no implied warranty with regard to defects which an examination ought in the circumstances to have revealed to him; and (c) an implied warranty can also be excluded or modified by course of dealing or course of performance or usage of trade. (4) Remedies for breach of warranty can be limited in accordance with the provisions of this Article on liquidation or limitation of damages and on contractual modification of remed
perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a contradiction by adding "unless such use was grossly negligent or willful" to a liability waiver. The original text was a blanket waiver, but the changed text implies liability could exist under certain conditions. The UCC snippet, particularly § 2-316, addresses the exclusion or modification of warranties. While this section focuses on warranties, the principle of clear and unambiguous disclaimers applies. The modified text introduces ambiguity by creating an exception to the waiver, making the scope of the disclaimer unclear. This contradicts the original intent and effectiveness of the liability waiver.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement constitutes the entire under standing between all the Parties and supersede s all previou s under standings, agreements, communications and representations, wheth er written or oral, concerni ng the discus sions by and between the Parties hereto and the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement constitutes the entire under standing between all the Parties and supersede s all previou s under standings, agreements, communications and representations, wheth er written or oral, concerni ng the discus sions by and between the Parties hereto and the Confidential Information, except for any verbal agreements made during the negotiation process.
perturbation.explanation: An integration clause (or entire agreement clause) is meant to ensure that the written contract represents the complete agreement. Excluding "verbal agreements made during the negotiation process" creates ambiguity and potential for disputes based on undocumented understandings. It undermines the purpose of the integration clause and increases the risk of conflicting interpretations.
perturbation.law_explanation: This section addresses the effect of integrated agreements on prior agreements. By explicitly excluding verbal agreements, the changed text directly contradicts the principle that a fully integrated agreement discharges prior agreements within its scope. This introduces ambiguity and opens the door for disputes based on parol evidence that the integration clause was meant to prevent.
perturbation.justification: The changed text undermines the purpose of an integration clause by explicitly excluding verbal agreements made during negotiations. This contradicts the principle that a fully integrated agreement discharges prior agreements within its scope. This is supported by the Law Explanation which states that this change introduces ambiguity and opens the door for disputes. While the scraped snippets are not directly relevant due to their poor quality, the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation both emphasize the contradiction that exists when verbal agreements are not superseded, and the original contract is changed to allow them. This undermines the integration clause. The exclusion creates a significant contradiction because it potentially allows prior verbal agreements to override the written contract, a direct violation of the clause's intent. Therefore, the existence of a legal contradiction is clear.
